does: This paper introduces ClawdGo, a framework for training autonomous AI agents to recognize and defend against security threats through endogenous (internal) awareness rather than relying solely on perimeter defenses. The framework consists of four main components: TLDT organizes security dimensions across three layers (Self-Defence, Owner-Protection, and Enterprise-Security); ASAT implements a self-play training loop where agents alternate between attacker, defender, and evaluator roles under curriculum scheduling; CSMA uses persistent multi-layer memory to accumulate defensive skills across sessions; and SACP formalizes the precision-recall tradeoff inherent in endogenous threat detection. The approach trains agents at inference time without requiring model modifications, enabling agents deployed on platforms like OpenClaw to reason about and resist prompt injection, memory poisoning, supply-chain attacks, and social engineering threats.
connects: This work builds on recent research into autonomous agent security and adversarial robustness. It extends prior work on prompt injection defenses and model alignment by shifting focus from external perimeter protection to training agents' internal threat judgment capabilities. The self-play training methodology draws from curriculum learning and adversarial training literature, while the memory architecture relates to work on persistent context in long-horizon agent tasks. The paper responds to the gap identified in existing security frameworks that address platform-level attacks but leave agent reasoning untrained, connecting to broader conversations about AI safety, interpretability of agent decision-making, and the limitations of purely defensive boundary approaches in multi-turn interactive systems.
enables: This framework opens several research directions for more robust autonomous agents. It demonstrates how endogenous security awareness can complement perimeter defenses, potentially enabling agents to operate safely in adversarial environments with reduced reliance on external monitoring. The ASAT curriculum approach could extend to other agent capabilities beyond security, suggesting general methods for teaching agents to reason about diverse challenge domains. The formalization of SACP points toward future work in calibrating trade-offs between false positives (over-defensive behavior) and false negatives (missed threats). Subsequent research could explore how security awareness transfers across different agent architectures, investigate methods for combining endogenous and exogenous defenses optimally, and develop evaluation benchmarks for agent security resilience analogous to existing adversarial robustness tests in ML.
